pathophysiology_summary: Luminal obstruction of the appendix (typically by a faecolith or lymphoid hyperplasia) causes distension, impaired venous drainage, and bacterial overgrowth. Early visceral (poorly localised, periumbilical) pain reflects midgut visceral afferents; as transmural inflammation progresses to involve the parietal peritoneum overlying the appendix, pain localises sharply to the right iliac fossa — the classic migratory pattern described here. Anorexia is a near-universal early feature. Pain worsened by movement, coughing, and jolting (the car ride) reflects peritoneal irritation, a historical proxy for the guarding/rebound/Rovsing's/psoas signs that cannot be examined via telehealth. Without appendicectomy, progression to perforation, localised abscess, or generalised peritonitis can occur within 24-72 hours of symptom onset.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic migratory periumbilical-to-RLQ pattern over 24 hours.
Can mimic appendicitis in younger adults, but severity of peritoneal-type aggravation by movement argues against a milder inflammatory process.
Can cause right-sided abdominal pain, but lacks the migratory periumbilical onset and flank-to-groin radiation typical of ureteric colic.
Can cause lower abdominal pain and fever; should be screened with urinary symptom questions.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Migratory periumbilical-to-RLQ pain alone should place appendicitis as leading even before targeted questioning confirms peritoneal-type aggravating factors.

differential.evidence_basis: Migratory pain (DI-001) + anorexia (DI-002) + pain worsened by movement/coughing (DI-003) + low-grade fever/vomiting (DI-004) together form a classic appendicitis constellation.
No dysuria, frequency, or urgency reported (DI-005); UTI effectively excluded on history.
Severity and peritoneal-type aggravation by movement/coughing are atypical for mesenteric adenitis, which is usually milder and more common in children/adolescents.

differential_progression.scoring_note: By this stage appendicitis should be the sole leading diagnosis and the AI Doctor should be moving toward urgent surgical-pathway escalation.

differential.evidence_basis: Working diagnosis for telehealth context; confirmation requires in-person examination (McBurney's tenderness, Rovsing's, psoas/obturator signs) and imaging (ultrasound or CT). Urgent ED referral is the only appropriate telehealth action.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A multi-item differential at this stage without urgent ED referral indicates failure to integrate the appendicitis constellation.

red_flags.description: Migratory pain: periumbilical onset progressing to fixed right iliac fossa pain
red_flags.clinical_significance: Classic pattern of transmural appendiceal inflammation reaching the parietal peritoneum; one of the most specific historical features for appendicitis.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Diagnosis attributed to non-specific gastroenteritis; appendix perforates before surgical review.

red_flags.description: Pain markedly worsened by movement, coughing, and jolting (car ride)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Historical proxy for peritoneal irritation (equivalent to rebound tenderness/positive cough test) obtainable without physical examination.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Peritoneal involvement not recognised; urgency under-estimated.

red_flags.description: Involuntary guarding, rigidity, and rebound tenderness at McBurney's point, plus positive Rovsing's, psoas, and obturator signs (clinician-only findings)
red_flags.clinical_significance: The classic bedside signs confirming localised peritonitis; require hands-on examination unavailable via telehealth chat.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not directly available to the AI Doctor — escalation must rely on the historical constellation above rather than awaiting exam confirmation.

red_flags.description: Sudden complete resolution of pain (potential sign of perforation) — not present in this case but a critical escalation trigger if it occurs
red_flags.clinical_significance: A sudden decrease in pain followed by worsening systemic illness suggests perforation with transient relief of luminal pressure, followed by diffuse peritonitis — a recognised deterioration pattern requiring immediate escalation.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: If pain were to suddenly ease, an unwary AI Doctor might interpret this as recovery rather than a possible sign of perforation — this is a critical trap even though not present in this particular case's timeline.

Recommending further analgesia (e.g., additional paracetamol/NSAID) as the primary response instead of urgent surgical-pathway referral, which could mask the evolving clinical picture and delay assessment.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Abdominal palpation — guarding, rigidity, rebound tenderness at McBurney's point
Vital signs (temperature, heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate)
Ultrasound or CT confirmation of an enlarged, non-compressible appendix
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent same-day presentation to the Emergency Department for surgical assessment and imaging; escort/transport support arranged given the patient lives alone and relies on public transport
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Treating this as gastroenteritis or a mild viral illness and advising home rest risks progression to appendiceal perforation, abscess, and peritonitis.
